summary: Blazing Cold Rain opener, monster Truckin'>Other One, rare Big Railroad Blues
blurb: The show bursts out with a high‑energy Cold Rain And Snow and stays on fire through Pigpen’s Turn On Your Lovelight. Highlights include a monster Truckin' → The Other One sequence, the debut of Sing Me Back Home and a razor‑sharp Big Railroad Blues. Overall performance is tight, spirited, and quintessential early‑‘71.
review: April 5, 1971 at Manhattan Center captured the Grateful Dead in top form. The first set kicks off with a blistering Cold Rain And Snow that immediately sets an urgent pace, then rolls through a playful Me And Bobby McGee (complete with Garcia’s signature yodel) and a tight Big Railroad Blues—later immortalized on the Skull & Roses album. Pigpen shines on Turn On Your Lovelight, delivering organ fireworks that drive the band into a soaring second set. The hallmark Truckin' → The Other One → Wharf Rat medley unfolds with relentless momentum, while Bertha, Deal, and the newly introduced Sing Me Back Home add depth to an already stellar run. Closing numbers like Not Fade Away, Goin’ Down The Road Feeling Bad, and a rousing Lovelight round out a setlist that feels both wild and impeccably rehearsed—an essential snapshot of the Dead’s early‑'70s peak.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across six of seven recordings (all marked 'positive' and praising the performance) with only one mixed review; no consistent negative comments about playing. Reviewers repeatedly call the show "blistering", "monster" and highlight multiple standout songs, justifying a high positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard mix that captures the full band energy, especially the Truckin' → The Other One sequence and Pigpen’s organ on Lovelight, with no dropouts.

key_highlights: Pigpen‑driven Turn On Your Lovelight fuels an early‑set surge
Monster Truckin' → The Other One → Wharf Rat medley showcases peak improvisational intensity
Rare Big Railroad Blues and debut of Sing Me Back Home appear in the setlist
Tight, crisp soundboard recording makes every instrument shine
song_highlights: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers a soaring, crystal‑clear tone—his solo on Big Railroad Blues is superb and his playful yodel on Bobby McGee adds a fun twist, while he sustains brilliance throughout the second set.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh lays down thunderous bass, highlighted by a driving line during Lovelight and an inspired ‘Philbomb’ solo that lifts the whole band.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals blend solidly with the group, adding depth on numbers like Me And Bobby McGee and supporting the high‑energy flow of the set.
band_performance.Brent: Pigpen’s organ is front‑and‑center—clear, powerful on Cold Rain And Snow and a driving force behind Turn On Your Lovelight, giving the show its distinctive early‑'71 flavor.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann provides a propulsive, tight drum foundation, especially evident in the relentless drive of Deal and the seamless transitions in the second set.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
